text: This video demonstrates the excision of a rectovaginal endometriotic nodule by a combined laparoscopic and vaginal approach. The patient is a thirty-seven years old woman, gravida one, para one. She had a previous cesarean section. She had severe pain in the right adnexia, radiating to the rectum at the time of menstruation. MRI demonstrated a three to four centimeter endometriotic nodule in the rectovaginal space. On speculum inspection, a four centimeter endometriotic nodule is apparent on the posterior fornix.

Laparoscopy reveals rectal involvement and obliteration of the cul-de-sac by endometriosis. The surgeon carefully grasps the peritoneum over the sacro-uterine ligament and opens it using an ultrasonic energy instrument. Dissection proceeds along the fatty tissue pathways between the rectum and the utero-sacral ligament, extending bilaterally deep to the level of the levator plate. The rectovaginal space is liberalized bilaterally below the adhesion point of the rectum to the uterus.

The surgeon completes the dissection, separating the rectum from the uterus and freeing the cul-de-sac, thereby opening the rectovaginal space. An endometriotic lesion on the rectal serosa is visible and palpated with the aspirator tip. The posterior vaginal fornix is elevated with the index finger, and a colpotomy incision is made over it to enter the vagina. The endometriotic nodule is palpated through the incision, with the ureter visible near the lesion site.

Through the vaginal route, the surgeon uses electrocautery to excise the endometriotic nodule completely. The nodule is grasped with two forceps and pushed back into the pelvis. The colpotomy incision is closed with two ovicral sutures placed vaginally, with laparoscopic monitoring to ensure the sutures are placed safely away from the ureter.

Photographs display the excised specimens of rectovaginal endometriotic nodules, illustrating the outcome of the vaginal excision following laparoscopic dissection and liberalization of the rectovaginal space.
